content.body: The process of reducing the total number of bits needed to represent information. You have original data → run through an **encoder** → smaller representation → later a **decoder** reconstructs it.

**Lossless compression:** The decoder can perfectly reconstruct the original data — every single bit is identical.

**Lossy compression:** Some information is permanently lost. The decoded data is only an approximation. Lecture 5 focuses entirely on **lossless** techniques.

Where $B_0$ = bits **before** compression, $B_1$ = bits **after** compression.

- Ratio of 2.0 → compressed file is half the original size
- Ratio of 1.0 → no compression happened at all
- Ratio < 1.0 → the "compressed" file is actually **bigger** (bad!)

If a symbol $s_i$ has probability $p_i$, the amount of information it carries is $I(s_i) = \log_2(1/p_i)$.

**Intuition:** A **rare** symbol is surprising — it carries **more** information. A **common** symbol is expected — it carries **less** information.

content.title: Self-Information Values
content.body:
- $p = 0.5$ → $I = \log_2(1/0.5) = \log_2(2) = \mathbf{1}$ bit
- $p = 0.25$ → $I = \log_2(1/0.25) = \log_2(4) = \mathbf{2}$ bits
- $p = 0.125$ → $I = \log_2(8) = \mathbf{3}$ bits

Every time the probability **halves**, the information goes up by **1 bit**.

The **average** information per symbol across the entire source:
$$H(S) = \sum_i p_i \log_2(1/p_i) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2(p_i)$$
Entropy is the **theoretical minimum** average bits per symbol. No lossless compression can ever do better.

content.title: Entropy: Two Key Cases
content.body:
**Case 1 — Uniform (worst for compression):**
An image where all 256 gray levels are equally likely ($p = 1/256$):
$$H = \log_2(256) = \mathbf{8 \text{ bits/symbol}}$$
You need a full 8 bits per pixel — **cannot compress at all**. When everything is equally likely, there is no pattern to exploit.

**Case 2 — Skewed (good for compression):**
An image with only two values where most pixels are one value → entropy ≈ **0.92 bits/symbol**. Far less than 8 bits — lots of compression potential.
**Key inequality:** $H \leq \bar{l} < H + 1$. Entropy is the lower bound. Huffman gets close but can never beat it. A memoryless source has each symbol independent of previous symbols.

flashcards.a: The process of reducing the total number of bits needed to represent information. Original data → encoder → smaller representation → decoder → reconstructed data.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between lossless and lossy compression?
flashcards.a: Lossless: the decoder perfectly reconstructs the original — every bit is identical. Lossy: some information is permanently lost — decoded data is only an approximation.

flashcards.q: What is the formula for compression ratio?
flashcards.a: CR = B₀ / B₁, where B₀ = bits before compression, B₁ = bits after. Ratio > 1 = smaller file. Ratio < 1 = file got bigger (bad!).
I(sᵢ) = log₂(1/pᵢ). Rare symbols carry more information. Common symbols carry less. Every time probability halves, information increases by 1 bit.
H = -Σ pᵢ × log₂(pᵢ). The average information per symbol. It is the theoretical minimum average bits/symbol. No lossless code can ever beat entropy.

flashcards.q: What is the entropy of a source with 256 equally likely symbols?
flashcards.a: H = log₂(256) = 8 bits. Maximum entropy — cannot be compressed at all. When everything is equally likely, there is no pattern to exploit.

flashcards.q: Why can a skewed distribution be compressed better than a uniform one?
flashcards.a: Skewed means some symbols are much more likely than others. The entropy is low (e.g., 0.92 bits vs 8 bits for uniform). Lower entropy = less average information = shorter codes possible.

flashcards.q: What is the key inequality for lossless coding?
flashcards.a: H ≤ l̄ < H + 1. The average code length l̄ is always at least entropy H, but Huffman guarantees it stays within 1 bit of H.
An information source where each symbol is independent — the current symbol doesn't depend on previous symbols. Huffman and Arithmetic Coding assume memoryless sources.

problems.q: Compute the self-information of symbols with probabilities: (a) 0.5, (b) 0.25, (c) 0.125.
problems.hint: I = log₂(1/p). Every time p halves, I increases by 1 bit.

problems.q: Compute the entropy of a source with symbols A ($p=0.5$), B ($p=0.25$), C ($p=0.25$).
Compute the entropy of 'banana'. Characters: a (3/6), n (2/6), b (1/6).
